Linking Social and Ecological Systems

                   CHAP TER 5.5


                   Linking Social and Ecological Systems
                   Wayne C. Zipperer, Wayde C. Morse, and Cassandra
                   Johnson Gaither




5.5.1 Introduction                                        have been used to integrate social and ecological sys-
                                                          tems. Second, we examine how system models have
On 16 November 2005 a water sample was taken              been used to link ecological systems with social sys-
from an urban stream in a metropolitan area in the        tems. Third, we introduce the concept of complex
southern United States and tested for the presence        adaptive systems (Gunderson & Holling 2002), as it
of E. coli. Although water samples from this and          may apply to urban landscapes, and finally we
other streams in the metropolitan area frequently         present a socio-ecological model (Morse 2007), based
registered over 15,000 colonies/100 ml, this particu-     on complex adaptive systems and structuration the-
lar sample is unique in that it registered a reading of   ory (Stones 2005), as a means to link social systems
70,000 colonies/100 ml, 350 per cent greater than the     with ecological systems.
200 colonies/100 ml—the Environment Protection
Agency’s standard for streams. The fetid floodwa-
ters in New Orleans from Hurricane Katrina, which
                                                          5.5.2 Socio-ecological integrators
had a contamination level of 10,000 colonies/100 ml
and attracted considerable public attention, were         Westley et al. (2002) eloquently discuss how ecologi-
cleaner than this stream at the time of sampling.         cal and social systems are quite different and that
   Although a number of factors can contribute to         the systems may not be as congruent as ecologists
this high reading, the stream consistently failed to      would like them to be. There are several reasons for
meet water quality standards throughout the year.         this difference. Ecological systems are characterized
In addition, children from the local neighbourhood        by time and space. Social systems are too character-
often played in the stream. Yet, presentations on         ized by time and space, but there is also a third
water quality issues and potential health hazards         dimension—‘structure of significance’ (Westley et al.
did not raise any concerns among the citizenry,           2002). Structure of significance refers to the ability of
news outlets, and policy-makers.                          humans to construct and manipulate symbols, prin-
   Obviously, there appears to be a disconnection         cipally words, thus collectively inventing a reality
between social and ecological systems as reflected by     that may or may not reflect true conditions. Human
the lack of concern by residents, natural resource        actions and decisions are influenced by this struc-
managers, and decision-makers to the degraded             ture of significance. In our water example, condi-
stream conditions. This disconnection suggests the        tions may not be perceived by individuals as badly
following question: How are social and ecological         as the actual condition of the stream, thus no action.
systems linked in urban landscapes and how does           Although there is ecological change, as reflected by
one begin to examine that linkage? In this chapter,       water quality, there is no social response.
we explore the linkage between the ecological and            This does not mean that social and ecological sys-
social systems of urban landscapes. First, we exam-       tems cannot be linked in an urban landscape. For

Research program (LTER) has taken a patch approach            Are they the same across a nation? Do they differ
to characterize social and ecological systems (Pickett        among nations? With further research, databases
et al. 1997). Pickett et al. (1997) proposed that by defin-   like PRIZM may provide insights into how social
ing the urban landscape through social and ecologi-           and ecological systems are integrated in urban and
cal patches one can overlay the different patch types         urbanizing landscapes.
and examine how social and ecological systems are                Cross-site analyses provide an opportunity to
related. To accomplish this approach, Grove et al.            compare how social and ecological systems are sim-
(2006) used PRIZM, a marketing classification sys-            ilar or dissimilar across urban areas. Both sense of
tem, to define social patches and vegetation cover to         place and land cover types have been used in cross-
characterize ecological conditions of riparian habitat,       site analyses to evaluate how socio-ecological sys-
private lands, and right-of-ways. PRIZM categorizes           tems vary among urban areas, and will be examined
people into lifestyle clusters based on household             in greater detail below.
education, income, occupation, race/ancestry, family
composition, and housing (Claritas 1999).
   Grove et al. (2006) report that standard variables,
                                                              5.5.2.1 Sense of place
such as income and education, did not explain vari-
ations in vegetation cover of the selected habitats.          The Millennium Ecosystem Assessment (2003)
Likewise, they observed that population density               defines ‘sense of place’ as one of the non-material,
was not a good predictor of vegetation cover, a               cultural services provided by ecosystems. It follows
social metric often used to characterize social condi-        then that when ecosystems or landscapes are altered
tions. Grove et al. (2006) did observe that lifestyle         to a measurable degree, the net gain or loss to cul-
behaviour was the best predictor for vegetation               tural services should be also altered, as would other
cover of private lands, and housing age was signifi-          provisioning or regulating services like food, water,
cantly associated with vegetation cover for each of           or climate. Arguably, a construct such as sense of
the selected habitats. They also reported that social         place is more difficult to gauge than other services
stratification was a better predictor of potential veg-       because the former depends to a greater extent on
etation cover, whereas lifestyle behaviour was a bet-         human perception. Still, these ecosystem services
ter predictor of present vegetation cover.                    are articulated strongly in instances of ecosystem
   The aspect of scale is especially problematic in           and landscape change. Bengston et al. (2005) remark:
socio-ecological research. For example, at the fine-          ‘[a]t the local level, . . . the core of the debate about
scale level, individual decisions affect the context in       sprawl . . . is the emotional impact people experience
which ecological structure and function occur. Yet,           when they lose places in their own communities
many of the policies regulating management deci-              they feel deeply attached to’.
sions are implemented at the broader scale. Grove                Sense of place is both a conceptual and an empiri-
et al. (2006) illustrate this interplay of scale of fine-     cal approach to assess humans’ emotive and cogni-
scale decisions and broad-scale management with               tive, non-tangible, cultural connection to place
respect to social systems. Lifestyle of landowners            (Relpf 1997). Fundamentally, sense of place refers to
influenced not only the vegetation on their property          people’s interpretation of a place and their resulting
but also on right-of-ways (managed lands), which              identification with the same. The domains of sense
are governed by a broad-scale management plan.                of place include place attachment (self-identity
   The use of PRIZM information to define social              related to place), place satisfaction (attitudes toward
patch types may be effective as a site specific analy-        place), place meanings (descriptive of why the place
sis; however, cross-site analyses may be limited              is important), and place characteristics (environ-
without further characterization of environmental             mental attributes) (Stedman 2002, 2003). We assume
attitudes, perceptions, and behaviours of marketing           that sense of places varies by perceiver and that
classes regionally, nationally, and internationally.          attachments are imparted to a place based on peo-
Do the environmental attitudes, perceptions, and              ple’s experiences with places. Meanings are not
behaviours of a marketing class vary regionally?              necessarily inherent in a place but are assigned and

                                                                    OUP CORRECTED PROOF – FINALS, 01/03/11, SPi




                                                                                        LINKING SOCIAL AND ECOLOGICAL SYSTEMS                      303


Table 5.5.1 Redman et al. (2004) proposed the following social patterns and processes to serve as core research areas for social analyses within
the National Science Foundation Long-Term Ecological Research Network

Demography: the growth, size, composition, distribution, and movement of human populations.
Technology change: the accumulated store of cultural knowledge about how to adapt to, use, and act on the biophysical environment and its
  material resources to satisfy human needs and wants.
Economic growth: the sets of institutional arrangements through which goods and services are produced and distributed.
Political and social institutions: enduring sets of ideas about how to accomplish goals recognized as important in a society. For instance,
  most societies have some form of family, religious, economic, educational, health, and political institutions that characterize its way of life.
Culture: culturally determined attitudes, beliefs, and values that purport to characterize aspects of collective reality, sentiments, and preferences
  of various groups at different scales, times, and places.
Knowledge and information exchange: the genetic and cultural communication of instructions, data, ideas, and so on.




rather leaves the identification of linkages to the                               These models tend to be structural in their orienta-
user. For example, Pickett et al. (1997) modified                              tion, focusing on system level patterns and processes
Machlis original model by enhancing the biophysi-                              such as institutions, demographics, policies, and
cal resources to represent ecosystem patterns and                              land-use patterns, and are known as system models.
processes, thus creating the framework to evaluate                             They often require detailed enumeration of causes
the effect of social structure on ecosystem structure                          and functional representations, and assume a linear-
andfun ction( Fig. 5.5.1).                                                     ity across a spatial scale (Parker et al. 2003). In general,
   Another modelling approach has been to link                                 these models are primarily biocentric—focusing prin-
social drivers with ecological systems. Alberti                                cipally on how ecological components are affected,
(2008) identifies important socio-ecological drivers                           this is to say the ecology in urban landscapes. In gen-
and recognizes the importance of spatio-temporal                               eral, they do not account directly for the effect of the

to social patterns and processes through relevant                              pally on how urbanization, through different socio-
interactions and feedbacks. Other models also                                  economic drivers, affects ecosystem patterns and
have been proposed. For instance, Grimm et al.                                 processes. That is not to say that socio-economic com-
(2000) developed a conceptual scheme that inte-                                ponents are not identified and recognized as being
grates ecological and social systems (Fig. 5.5.3).                             affected too (see Grimm et al. 2000), but rather that the

et al.’s (2000) approach is that the feedback loop                             changing socio-economic systems are generally not
within the social system is directly linked to land-                           quantified. For instance, in our water quality exam-
use and ecosystem patterns and processes. In other                             ple, we can identify and quantify those anthropogenic
words, they propose that in order to study social                              factors creating the condition leading to degraded
systems within an urban landscape, knowledge                                   water quality, but we have not identified changes in
and information about the ecological and social                                social responses to this condition. This is understand-
systems is a prerequisite, thus directly linking                               able considering ecologists and biologists are using
social patterns and processes to ecological patterns                           the models to study ecological patterns and processes
and processes. Yli-Pelkonen and Niemelä (2005)                                 under different land management conditions and
have applied Grimm et al.’s (2000) model to guide                              social contexts—the ecology in urban landscapes.
urban planning in making recommendations for                                      Another set of models defining socio-ecological
biological conservation of natural areas within an                             systems are called agent-based models. This set of
urban landscape. A more detailed description on                                models focuses on individuals or agents and their
how each of these models differ is described by                                decision-making processes and actions (Parker et al.
Alberti (2008).                                                                2003). Agents are perceived to have control over

304      URBAN ECOLOGY




                                                     Human Social System

                          SOCIAL                            SOCIAL CYCLES                        SOCIAL ORDER
                          INSTITUTIONS                      Physiological                        Identity       Social
                          Health                            Individual                           • Age          status
                          Justice                           Environmental                         • Gender      • Wealth
                          Faith                             Organizational                         • Class       • Power
                          Commerce                          Institutional                           • Clan        • Status
                          Education                                                              Norms          Knowledge
                          Leisure                                                                • Informal     • Territory
                          Sustenance                                                              • Formal
                          Government
                          Substenance




                                                          Resource System
                          CULTURAL                                                                 SOCIOECONOMIC
                          RESOURCES                                                                RESOURCES
                          Organizations                                                            Information
                          • Beliefs                                                                Population
                           • Myths                                                                 • Labor
                                                                                                    • Capital




                                                     Ecosystem Resources
                               ECOSYSTEM PROCESSES                             ECOSYSTEM PATTERNS
                               Energy           Nutrients                      Species                 Patch Mosaic
                               • Assimiluated   • Source                       • Source                • Configuration
                                • Thermal        • Form                         • Life History          • Disturbance
                                 • Source         • Concentration                • Evolution             • Context
                               Water            Material                       Soils                   Vegetation
                               • Path           • Toxics                       • Texture               • Structure
                                • Rate           • Legacies                     • Chemistry             • Composition
                                 • Quality        • Biomass                      • Carbon                • Regeneration
                                                   • Trace Gas
                               Air
                               • Quality




Figure 5.5.1 A conceptual model illustrating components within a human ecosystem and general linkages between those components, as
proposed by Pickett et al. (1997). With kind permission from springer Science+Business Media

                  DRIVERS                                                                       PROCESSES
                  Population growth                                                             Runoff/erosion
                  Economic growth                                                               Nutrient cycles

                  Climate                                                                       Community development

                                                         Natural productivity
                                                         Salmon runs

                                                         Community dynamics
                                                         Human behavior

model allows for understanding of feedback that            we need to expand our understanding of CAS by
links changing ecological conditions to decision           linking social and ecological systems in a Structuration
making. How do actors learn from past decisions?           of Complex Adaptive Systems (SoCAS) framework
What information is taken into account when mak-           (see Morse 2007).
ing decisions? Is ecological information taken into
account? If it is, where did this information come         Structurationo fC omplexA daptiveSys tems
from? How accurate is the information? How do we           Central to a SoCAS framework are elements of
change the structures that influence future deci-          structuration theory from the social sciences (Stones
sions? In essence, how do we respond to environ-           2005), those of complex adaptive systems outlined
mental feedbacks?                                          above (Levin 1998; Gunderson & Holling 2002), and
                                                           the theory of hierarchical patch dynamics (Wu and
                                                           Loucks 1995) from the ecological sciences (Morse
                                                           2007). Both social and ecological theories are used
5.5.3.1 Complex adaptive systems
                                                           to provide guidance because the drivers of these
The processes of structural change to both social and      systems operate differently across spatial and tem-
ecological systems are examined by focusing on the         poral scales. Human actors act with foresight,
cyclical process of human actions and interactions         reflexivity, and can communicate those ideas into
with the environment. The approach is based on the         the future, while ecosystems do not (Westley et al.
premise that urban landscapes are complex adaptive         2002). For the framework, the social and ecological
systems (see Gunderson & Holling 2002). In complex         CAS mirror each other and are linked at the point
adaptive systems (CAS), the interactions of lower          where human actions and interactions with the
level components result in emergent patterns at            environmento ccur( Fig. 5.5.4).
higher levels that, in turn, feedback to influence            Structuration theory frames ‘the interaction of
future lower level interactions (Levin 1998). It is        structure and agency across scales [that] must be
through this cyclic process of interactions and feed-      the centerpiece of a dynamic understanding of peo-
back that CAS self-organizes, often into nested sys-       ple–environment interaction’ (Scoones 1999).
tems (Levin 1998). CAS are also characterized by           Structuration theory avoids both an overly objec-
high levels of uncertainty, cross-scale interactions,      tive structural approach and an exaggerated empha-
threshold effects, and the possibility of multiple equi-   sis of subjectivist, agent-based approaches by
libria (Gunderson & Holling 2002). With a founda-          focusing on their interaction (Stones 2005). Human
tion in CAS, it is clear that understanding the            action is viewed as a continuous flow of conduct
feedback between and within social and ecological          (Giddens 1984). Structure is seen as both ‘the
systems is critical if we are to manage for the resil-     medium and outcome of the conduct it recursively
ience of these systems and their sustainability.           organizes’ (Giddens 1984). Structure enters into the
Adaptive environmental assessment and manage-              constitution of the agent as a medium (internal
ment was developed as a flexible approach to address       structure) and from there into the practices that the
uncertainty and to provide a framework for active          agent produces as an outcome (external structure)
learning (Gunderson & Holling 2002). One of the            (Stones 2005). Structures that are the outcome of
central tenets of adaptive management is learning,         one period of conduct (actions, activities) become
and the learning is dependent upon processing of           the medium for the next round of agents’ conduct
information in a formalized manner through experi-         (Stones 2005). Through recursive social conduct,
                                                                           OUP CORRECTED PROOF – FINALS, 01/03/11, SPi




                                                                                                LINKING SOCIAL AND ECOLOGICAL SYSTEMS                      307



      Social systems as            A
                                                                                                                               Patch mosaic ecological
      conditions for action                                                                                                    context

                                                                       B


                              Actors with resources and capabilities                          Patch level ecological context




         Time                                                                Action:                                                     Time
                                                             C
                                                                      Social and Ecological



                               New perceptions, attitudes,                                    New patch level ecological
                               resources and capabilities of actors                           patterns and processes
                                                                                D

                                                                                                                               New (renewed) mosaic
      New (renewed) social systems                                                                                             level ecological patterns
      as products of action                                                                                                    and processes


                                        A. Knowledge of social conditions and the capability to act on those conditions
                                           varies by individual actor.
                                        B. Ecological knowledge at the landscape and patch level is considered in
                                           land-use choices. This knowledge is not perfect. Resulting in both
                                           acknowledged and unacknowledged conditions.
                                        C. Human actions include both land conservation and land-use change
                                           decisions. The action may be considered a disturbance in ecological systems.
                                        D. There are both intended and unintended consequences of action (land-use
                                           decisions) on social and ecological systems at the scale of the individual and
                                           the system.


Figure 5.5.4 A conceptual model linking social decision-making processes with landscape dynamics to characterize the effect of land-use on
social and ecological systems. This model is nested hierarchically within larger systems. With permission from The University of California Press



structures influence the activity of individuals, who                                Some of this information is ecological (B) and is not
in turn, transform or reaffirm those same structures                                 always perfect (dashed lines). Other information
constantly producing and reproducing society                                         reflects the extent to which the decision-maker knows
(Kondrat 2002).                                                                      about external social structures and how to ‘make
   Social systems (e.g. markets, governments) can                                    things happen’ within the system (A). The decision-
be thought of as the patterns of social relations, or                                maker takes action based on this knowledge (A, B)
regularized social practices, that stretch across                                    and incorporates their capabilities (resources at their
time and space produced by the process of struc-                                     disposal) to take action.
turation (Giddens 1984). They are the complex,                                          The black box of actor decision-making in
entrenched, and powerful networks of relation-                                       Structuration includes, 1) their motivation to
ships, behaviors, beliefs, interactions, rules, and                                  action, 2) the rationalization of action and knowl-
resources, and are both temporally and spatially                                     edge, and 3) their ability to reflexively monitor
contingent (Kondrat 2002). Furthermore, they are                                     their action (Giddens 1984). Motivations are the
integrated with other social systems hierarchi-                                      wants and desires of an individual and are their
cally, across space and over time. Structures are                                    overall plans for conduct. Rationalization of action
considered to be both enabling and constraining                                      includes the agent’s knowledge and how to pro-
of agents actions (Giddens 1984).                                                    ceed to obtain their intended outcomes. This

mation is an input for the decision process (Fig. 5.5.4).                            always the possibility of unacknowledged condi-

308    URBAN ECOLOGY


tions and unintended consequences. Reflexive           review of the application of multiagent system
monitoring of action includes the agents’ contin-      models to simulate land-use change.
ual monitoring of actions and the consequences of
their actions, and of the actions of others. This      5.5.4 Summary
monitoring can be for both social and environ-
mental consequences. This understanding of the         We began this chapter by presenting a scenario
individual’s decision-making process matches           involving a highly contaminated stream and the
nicely with that of adaptive management. The           lack of political or environmental response to it. We
focus on knowledge provides a framework to             ended the chapter by discussing ways in which to
emphasize conservation learning, the use of infor-     model social and ecological data. During this course,
mation, and the development of an ecological aes-      we presented different techniques and models cur-
thetic. ‘As knowledge about both social and            rently being used to evaluate the integration of social
ecological systems is often imperfect and the          and ecological systems. Throughout the discussion,
cumulative and cascading consequences of our           the underlying assumption is that the ecology of the
actions within and across both systems are             system can be derived for urban settlement along an
extremely complex and difficult to predict, explicit   urban to rural gradient only through long-term
identification of these knowledge systems (or lack     monitoring of both social and ecological compo-
thereof) is a critical feature for a framework of      nents across multiple scales. Through the long-term
linked social ecological CAS’ (Morse 2007). Our        monitoring of social and ecological systems, we will
model, and the others using this integrated            be able to build into these models the nuances of the
approach, are well-adapted to capturing the inter-     complexity of our urban systems, thus potentially
dependencies, heterogeneity, and nested hierar-        linking changes to social patterns and process to
chies among agents and their environments              ecological patterns and processes. In doing so, we
characterizing urban landscapes (Parker et al.         begin to take the necessary steps to identify factors
2003). Parker et al. (2003) provides an excellent      affecting the sustainability of urban landscapes.
